OKSM (Общероссийский классификатор стран мира) is the Russian All-Russian Classifier of Countries of the World. It uses 3-digit numeric codes to represent countries, dependent territories, and special areas of geographical interest. The classifier is based on ISO 3166-1 numeric codes but may include additional codes specific to Russian classification needs. Common codes include 643 for Russia, 276 for Germany, 840 for United States, and 826 for United Kingdom.

doc: A Bitcoin address is only a hash, so the sender can't provide a full public key in scriptPubKey. The script verifies that the provided public key does hash to the hash in scriptPubKey, and then it also checks the signature against the public key.

doc: OCLC assigns a unique control number (referred to as an "OCN" for "OCLC Control Number") to each new bibliographic record in the WorldCat. Numbers are assigned serially, and as of mid-2013 over a billion OCNs had been created. In September 2013, the OCLC declared these numbers to be in the public domain, removing a perceived barrier to widespread use of OCNs outside OCLC itself.[42] The control numbers link WorldCat's records to local library system records by providing a common reference key for a record across libraries

doc: The International Standard Serial Number (ISSN) is an eight-digit serial number used to uniquely identify a serial publication, such as a magazine. The ISSN is especially helpful in distinguishing between serials with the same title. name: International Standard Serial Number (ISSN)

doc: The International Standard Recording Code (ISRC) is an international standard code for uniquely identifying sound recordings and music video recordings. The code was developed by the recording industry and is officially designated as the ISO 3901 standard. An ISRC identifies a particular recording, not the work (composition and lyrical content) itself. Therefore, different recordings, edits, and remixes of the same work should have different ISRCs.

name: Code SIRET par nom de champ
doc: FR datatype for 'Code SIRET par nom de champ' (siret) from rules in context 'finances'. The SIRET (Système d'Identification du Répertoire des Établissements) is a unique 14-digit identifier assigned to each business establishment in France. It consists of two parts: the first 9 digits are the SIREN number (identifying the legal entity or company), and the last 5 digits are the NIC (Numéro Interne de Classement), where the first 4 digits identify the specific establishment and the last digit is a check digit calculated using the Luhn algorithm. The SIRET number is used for official identification and registration of business establishments in France.

The Official Journal reference is a unique identifier used to reference notices and documents published in the Official Journal of the European Union. The format consists of the prefix 'OJ/S' followed by an optional space, then 1 to 4 digits representing the notice number, a forward slash, and 1 to 4 digits representing the year or sequence number.

name: NUTS region code
doc: EU datatype for 'NUTS region code' (nutsregion) from rules in context 'government'. The NUTS (Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics) region code is a hierarchical classification system used by the European Union to divide its territory for statistical purposes. The code format consists of 2 uppercase letters representing the country code, followed by 1 to 3 alphanumeric characters representing the region level (NUTS 1, 2, or 3).

name: TED notice id
doc: EU datatype for 'TED notice id' (tednoticeid) from rules in context 'government'. The TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) notice ID is a unique identifier assigned to procurement notices published in the Tenders Electronic Daily database, which is the online version of the Supplement to the Official Journal of the European Union. The identifier format consists of a 4-digit year followed by a hyphen and then 1 to 8 digits representing the notice number within that year.

The Contract Award Notice ID is a unique identifier assigned to contract award notices published in the Official Journal of the European Union. The identifier format consists of a 4-digit year, followed by '/S', then 1 to 3 digits representing the supplement number, a hyphen, and finally 1 to 5 digits representing the notice number within that supplement.

European Unique Identifier (EUID)
doc: EU datatype for 'European Unique Identifier (EUID)' (euid) from rules in context 'finances'. The European Unique Identifier (EUID) is a unique identifier format used within the European Union. It consists of a 2-letter uppercase country code (ISO 3166-1 alpha-2) followed by 4 to 18 alphanumeric characters (letters and/or digits), resulting in a total length of 6 to 20 characters. This identifier format is commonly used for financial and business identification purposes across EU member states.

doc: EU datatype for 'VAT number' (euvat) from rules in context 'finances'. The EU VAT identification number (VAT ID) is a unique identifier assigned to businesses for VAT purposes within the European Union. The format consists of a 2-letter country code (ISO 3166-1 alpha-2) followed by 6 to 12 alphanumeric characters. The exact format and length vary by member state, but all follow the general pattern of country code plus alphanumeric identifier.
